description: Technical lesson log for deeper side-paths explored during the Pokemon Card Display project.

lessons.problem: How to render pokemon cards dynamically without relying only on innerHTML string templating.
lessons.pattern: createElement -> configure node -> render inner content -> appendChild
notes: createElement creates a DOM node in memory.
The node is not yet visible in the page after creation.
appendChild inserts the node into the DOM tree, making it visible inside its parent container.
dataset can attach useful metadata like data-name without hardcoding it directly into the HTML string.
For this small project, innerHTML would also have been a valid practical solution. This lesson was explored to understand the DOM more deeply, not because the simpler approach was wrong.

lessons.topic: Decorative UI via data attributes and pseudo-elements
lessons.problem: How to display a pokemon primary type badge on cards without adding extra HTML elements.
lessons.pattern: dataset in JS -> CSS attribute selector -> ::after pseudo-element
notes: JavaScript attached semantic metadata to the card via data-type.
CSS used attribute selectors to react to that metadata.
The ::after pseudo-element created a decorative badge without modifying the HTML structure.
This pattern works well for decorative UI elements such as badges, icons, or status indicators.
It becomes less appropriate when the information represents core content rather than decoration.
In this project, the pattern was later removed because pokemon types are real content in the dialog view and therefore better rendered as HTML.

lessons.topic: Array fallback pattern for missing data
lessons.problem: How to handle cases where pokemon data might be missing or incomplete without breaking the rendering logic.
lessons.pattern: function arrayFallback(arr, fallback) { return arr && arr.length > 0 ? arr : [fallback]; }
notes: This pattern provides a simple way to ensure that the rendering logic always has an array to work with, even if the original data is missing or empty.
It prevents errors that would occur if the code tried to iterate over undefined or null values.
The fallback value can be customized based on the expected data structure, allowing for graceful degradation of the UI.
In this project, it was used to ensure that every pokemon card could display at least one type, even if the API response was incomplete.

lessons.topic: Grid rows for stable card layout
lessons.problem: How to place pokemon types, image, and name in a clear vertical card structure without the layout feeling jumpy when content varies.
lessons.pattern: parent grid defines vertical zones -> top row for types -> middle row for image -> bottom row for name
notes: Grid is useful when the card should be split into clear vertical sections.
The top row can hold the type icons, the middle row the image, and the bottom row the pokemon name.
Flex inside .poke-types still handles the horizontal arrangement of the icons.
Grid and Flex are not opposites: Grid organizes the large page or card structure, Flex often organizes small groups inside one area.
min-height on .poke-types does not control the icon direction. It only reserves a minimum vertical space for that row.
This helps cards feel calmer when one pokemon has one type and another has two types.
A useful mental model is: Grid defines the shelves, Flex arranges the objects on one shelf.
